places.name: Airlie Beach

visits.text: Airlie Beach is the gateway to the 74 Whitsunday Islands, an archipelago in the heart of the Great Barrier Reef. The town itself has a free outdoor artificial lagoon (open 24/7) allowing year-round safe swimming—Queensland tropical coastal waters are unsuitable October to May due to box jellyfish (Chironex fleckeri). The vast majority of Whitsundays excursions depart from Airlie Beach.

Whitehaven Beach, on Whitsunday Island (the archipelago's largest), is a 7 km beach with 98-99% pure silica sand—an extraordinarily rare composition worldwide. This white sand doesn't retain heat even in full sun, and squeaks slightly underfoot when walking. The Ngaro, traditional people of the Whitsundays, called it 'the sands that whisper.' The beach is accessible only by boat or flight from Airlie Beach—no road reaches it.

Hill Inlet Lookout, at Whitehaven Beach's northern end from Tongue Bay (10-15 min walk from the landing), is one of Australia's most photographed panoramas. At mid-tide, estuary currents shift the silica sand through turquoise water, creating ever-changing patterns of white and blue swirls that never repeat identically. The Inlet is listed in the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park.

activities.text: 2-day/1-night sailing cruise in the Whitsundays from Airlie Beach—the most complete way to explore the islands, sleep anchored in a cove, snorkel coral reefs and reach Whitehaven Beach at dawn before day-tripper arrival. Charter catamarans and sailboats are numerous with packages from 1 to 7 nights.

places.name: Albany

visits.text: The National ANZAC Centre, opened in 2014 on Mount Clarence in Albany Heritage Park, is Australia's most significant museum dedicated to the Anzac legend. It was from Albany Harbour that the first two ANZAC convoys departed—41,265 Australian and New Zealand soldiers on 38 ships, most of whom would never see their homeland again. Visitors assume the identity of one of 32 documented soldiers or nurses, following their journey from enlistment through to the final fate revealed at the visit's end.

Albany's historic centre, the first settlement established in Western Australia in 1827, clusters around Stirling Terrace and its limestone colonial buildings. Albany is WA's oldest town—its natural harbour at King George Sound, one of the largest and most protected on the continent, gave it decisive strategic importance. The Residency Museum, the replica brig Amity, and the fortifications of Princess Royal Fortress on Mount Adelaide complete the historical tour.

Middleton Beach is Albany's town beach—a long ribbon of sand sheltered by the Flinders Peninsula headland, with calmer waters than the southern coast. The waterfront promenade links the beach to Ellen Cove and Emu Point jetty. At dusk, the sun sets over the Stirling Ranges to the north.

The Gap and Natural Bridge are Torndirrup National Park's two most spectacular formations, 20 km from Albany. The Gap is a 25-metre-deep channel carved through granite by the Southern Ocean—a stainless steel platform overhangs the abyss 40 metres above the roaring sea. A few dozen metres away, the Natural Bridge is a granite arch sculpted by millions of years of erosion.

The historic Frenchman Bay whaling station (Cheynes Beach Whaling Station) operated until 1978—the last of its kind in Australia. Now transformed into a museum, it houses the whaler Cheynes IV (decommissioned in 1979) and interactive installations tracing the history of whaling in the Great Southern. Whale skeletons, harpoons, and flensing boilers provide a striking glimpse of an industry now vanished.

Two Peoples Bay Nature Reserve, 35 km east of Albany, is one of Western Australia's most precious ornithological sanctuaries. It's home to the noisy scrub-bird, a species thought extinct until its rediscovery in 1961. Little Beach, at the heart of the reserve, is a pink granite cove with impossibly turquoise water—accessible after a short 1.5 km walk from the car park.

activities.text: Climb to the summit of Mount Clarence via the Convoy Walk to discover the Desert Mounted Corps Memorial and Padre White Lookout. The 360° panorama encompasses King George Sound, Albany town, and the Torndirrup cliffs to the south—the same waters contemplated by ANZAC soldiers departing for Gallipoli.

Stroll through the historic Stirling Terrace precinct to see 19th-century limestone buildings. The local market, art galleries, and independent boutiques make this one of the best-preserved town centres on the south coast.

Explore the Torndirrup Blowholes (1.6 km return, 78 steps): cracks in the granite plateau connect with the sea below and eject water and air with each wave. The effect is spectacular during winter's heavy swells.

Swim at Little Beach in Two Peoples Bay Reserve. After the approach walk (1.5 km return), the reward is a pink granite beach framed by cliffs, whose calm, crystalline waters invite swimming. Arrive early morning to avoid crowds.

Hike the Stony Hill Walk Trail (500 m accessible loop), a flat circuit offering 360° views over Torndirrup Park, Peak Head, and the Southern Ocean. Short but panoramic—one of the area's best viewpoints accessible without effort.

places.name: Beagle Bay

visits.text: Sacred Heart Church at Beagle Bay, completed in 1918, is one of Australia's most extraordinary religious buildings. Built entirely from bricks fired on-site by mission residents—60,000 bricks hand-molded from local clay—its altar is inlaid with mother-of-pearl, cowries, volutes, and olives gathered from peninsula reefs. Traditional Nyulnyul, Nimanborr, and Bardi motifs blend with Stations of the Cross imagery. The Bardi Jawi people have lived on these lands for tens of thousands of years.

Middle Lagoon, halfway between Beagle Bay and Cape Leveque, is a lagoon campsite with calm waters protected by a sandstone reef. Red pindan cliffs drop straight into turquoise water—the archetypal Dampier Peninsula landscape. Fishing here is famously productive.

activities.text: On the drive from Broome, stop at James Price Point, 60 km north, to see dinosaur footprints on beach sandstone slabs at low tide. Theropods, ornithopods, and sauropods left their tracks in what was then a tropical river delta in the Cretaceous. The site is also known for the successful fight against Woodside Energy's industrial gas project in 2012—a national campaign that preserved this coast.

Stop at Lombadina community for a guided tour with Bardi Jawi traditional custodians: traditional net fishing, medicinal plant gathering, and wood carving demonstrations. The community also offers boat tours into the mangroves.

places.name: Bell Gorge

visits.text: Dalmanyi (Bell Gorge) is the most spectacular gorge on the Gibb River Road. Bell Creek plunges 100 meters from the Wunaamin Miliwundi Ranges in a cascading waterfall before emptying into a deep pool ringed by horseshoe cliffs. The descent to the swimming hole is steep and rocky.
Lennard River Gorge (Darrambal) is reached via a 3 km return walk from the carpark. The falls — impressive early in the dry season — are visible from the lookout perched above the deep gorge.

activities.text: Hike to Bell Gorge (4 km return, 1.5–3h depending on pace). The trail follows Bell Creek to the waterfall overlook, then you can scramble down the rocks to swim in the lower pool — one of the Kimberley's finest swimming holes.

On the way to Bell Gorge, detour to Galvans Gorge, just 1 km off the track. An easy trail follows the creek to a horseshoe pool with Aboriginal rock art, an ancient boab, and the famous rope swing. One of the Gibb's most accessible stops.

places.name: Broome

visits.text: Cable Beach stretches 22.5 kilometers northwest of Broome—one of Australia's longest white sand beaches. Named after the undersea telegraph cable laid in 1889 to connect Broome to Java and the wider world, the beach experiences tides reaching up to 9 meters—among the highest anywhere on Earth. At extreme low tide, theropod and sauropod dinosaur footprints emerge from the red Broome Sandstone—the only record of Western Australia's Early Cretaceous dinosaur fauna, preserved 130 million years ago.

Gantheaume Point, 6 km south of Broome, is a red sandstone promontory jutting into the Indian Ocean. Its limestone rocks contain fossilized tracks from 21 different dinosaur species—theropods, sauropods, ornithopods—preserved in the Broome Sandstone. The trackways extend along 80 km of coastline and were inscribed on the Australian National Heritage List in 2011. At high tide, plaster casts mounted on the rock let you visualize the prints even when the originals are submerged. In Yawuru tradition, these footprints mark the path of Marala, the Emu Man of the Dreaming.

Broome's Chinatown, centered on Dampier Terrace, is the historic heart of the pearling town. At the turn of the 20th century, Broome supplied 80% of the world's mother-of-pearl, and its streets teemed with Japanese, Chinese, Malays, Filipinos, Timorese, and Europeans in a cosmopolitan chaos unique in Australia. Sun Pictures, opened in 1916 by pearl master Ted Pearler, is the world's oldest operating outdoor cinema. Johnny Chi Lane's interpretive panels bring to life this frontier town in the era of opium dens and luggers.

Broome's Japanese Cemetery, the largest of its kind in Australia, holds more than 900 headstones carved in Japanese characters—testament to divers who died from decompression accidents, cyclones, or sharks. In 1908, a single cyclone sank 41 luggers and killed 40 men in one night. The central obelisk was erected by the survivors. Half the gravestones bear the names of young men from Wakayama Prefecture—a poor coastal region south of Osaka whose sons made up the majority of divers.

The Pearl Luggers Museum on Dampier Terrace displays two restored early-20th-century luggers complete with copper helmet diving suits, lead boots, and woolen dive gear. The divers' accounts—some made up to 50 dives a day at 40 meters depth for four months straight—reveal the brutal conditions of an industry that shaped the town. In 1900, 400 luggers were anchored in Roebuck Bay.

Broome Bird Observatory, on the shores of Roebuck Bay east of town, monitors one of the world's most important shorebird feeding grounds. More than 155,000 waders—including godwits, sandpipers, and plovers—make the annual migration from Siberian Arctic and northeast Asia to Roebuck Bay, a journey of 10,000 km. The bay is a Ramsar-listed wetland of international importance.

At extreme low tide in Roebuck Bay, the wrecks of flying boats sunk during the Japanese bombing raid of March 3, 1942 rise through the shallows—16 aircraft destroyed in a single day, making this the second-deadliest air raid on Australia after Darwin. Most victims were Dutch refugees fleeing Java. Some wrecks remain visible in optimal conditions from the jetty.

Minyirr Park, the coastal reserve adjoining Cable Beach, is co-managed by the Shire of Broome and the Yawuru people. Interpreted trails explain how the Yawuru read the seven seasons of their calendar—Mankala (flowering season), Laja (cold and dry), Barrgana (windy season)—from the behavior of plants and animals. Several species of tree snakes, monitors, and blue-tongued lizards inhabit the dune vegetation.

activities.text: Walk the Jetty to Jetty Heritage Trail (2.5 km) from Streeter's Jetty to Town Beach. This self-guided route, narrated by the Yawuru app, traces 13 cultural and historical sites—the colonial jetty, remnants of the pearling town, and Yawuru sacred spaces. Download the app before you go to hear the voices of traditional custodians as you walk.

At sunset, join the iconic Cable Beach camel ride—a Broome institution. Camels walk the beach facing the Indian Ocean in the golden evening light. The experience lasts about an hour; several operators offer late-afternoon departures directly from the beach.

Take a guided tour of Willie Creek Pearl Farm or Cygnet Bay Pearl Farm, less than an hour from Broome. These working pearl farms explain the full cycle of culturing Pinctada maxima—the world's largest pearl oyster—from seeding to harvest. Broome produces around 70% of the world's large cultured pearls. Pearl meat tastings and grading sessions are offered.

On full moon nights (March to October), the 'Staircase to the Moon' phenomenon occurs over Roebuck Bay's mudflats: moonlight reflecting off the exposed tidal flats creates the illusion of a golden staircase rising into the sky. Town Beach night markets set up on these evenings.

Browse Broome Courthouse Markets (Saturday morning) or Chinatown markets—local produce, Aboriginal art, cultured pearls, Asian specialties. Last supply stop before heading to Derby and the Gibb River Road.

Return to Derby (220 km, 2.5 hours) to join the Gibb River Road, or fly back to Perth from Broome Airport. Broome is connected to Perth by daily flights (2h10) and to Darwin via connections through Port Hedland or Perth.

places.name: Byron Bay

visits.text: Cape Byron is the Australian mainland's easternmost point. Cape Byron Lighthouse (1901), Australia's most powerful with a 45 km range, stands atop the headland. The 3.7 km coastal trail from Main Beach to the cape traverses rainforest-covered cliffs and offers exceptional Pacific Ocean views. Between June and November, humpback whales and dolphins are regularly spotted from the cape during their annual migration.

Byron Bay is continental Australia's easternmost town, known for its laid-back atmosphere and arts scene. Its east and northeast-facing beaches catch the north NSW coast's best surf swells—Main Beach, Belongil and The Pass are the most popular breaks. Wategos Beach, at the cape's base, is a sheltered cove surf spot ideal for beginners.

activities.text: Byron Bay market, alternative and artisanal, takes place the first Sunday of each month at Butler Street Reserve. Ceramics, jewelry, ethical clothing, local produce and food trucks compose one of the east coast's most authentic market atmospheres—beloved by travelers and locals. A smaller market runs every Thursday at the primary school.

Surf or yoga lessons on Main Beach—Byron Bay is considered Australia's yoga and wellness capital, with more studios per square kilometer than any Australian city. Surf schools offer beginner lessons supervised from the patrolled beaches.

places.name: Cairns

visits.text: The Great Barrier Reef, UNESCO site since 1981, is the world's largest coral system at 2,300 km long, with 2,900 individual reefs and over 900 islands. It harbors 1,500 fish species, 400 coral species, 4,000 mollusk species, 30 cetacean species and 6 of the world's 7 sea turtle species. Visible from space, it's the planet's largest living structure—according to IUCN, if only one coral site worldwide deserved World Heritage listing, it would be the Great Barrier Reef. Excursions from Cairns reach the Outer Reef in 90 minutes.

Green Island, 27 km from Cairns (45 min by catamaran), is a 12-hectare coral island—a cay entirely composed of coral and sand. Its dense littoral forest shelters herons and frigatebirds, and its fringing reef accessible by swimming from the beach is one of the best snorkeling spots reachable without specialized equipment. Glass-bottom semi-submersibles allow reef observation without getting wet.

Kuranda Scenic Railway (1891) is one of Australia's most scenic train journeys: 34 km of hand-built track constructed between 1888 and 1891 through Barron Gorge National Park rainforest, with 15 tunnels and 40 bridges. The 1h45 journey from Cairns reaches the tropical village of Kuranda at 330 m elevation. Skyrail Rainforest Cableway (7.5 km) allows return above the canopy with intermediate descents into the forest.

Daintree National Park, 110 km north of Cairns, is the world's oldest continuous wet tropics at 135 million years—older than Amazonian rainforests. The UNESCO-listed forest (Wet Tropics of Queensland, 1988) descends to the sea at Cape Tribulation, the only place on Earth where two UNESCO sites meet: the world's oldest rainforest and the Great Barrier Reef. Mossman Gorge offers swimming in crystal-clear waters at the forest's heart.

Tjapukai Aboriginal Cultural Park at Caravonica (15 min from Cairns) is the main cultural center of the Djabugay people, traditional custodians of the lands around Cairns. Artists and elders transmit traditional techniques—spear throwing, boomerang throwing, bark painting, earth oven cooking—in a setting respectful of cultural transmission. Daily performances afternoon and evening.

activities.text: Diving or snorkeling on the Outer Reef from Cairns—this is the journey's central experience. Major operators (Silverswift, Reef Magic, Great Adventures) offer full-day trips with fast catamaran transfer to 2-3 reef sites, equipment included, certified instructors for beginners. Introductory dives available without PADI certification. Reef around Hastings, Moore and Flynn reefs is in good condition.

Evening stroll through the Cairns Esplanade Night Markets—permanent night market open nightly until 11pm with Australian crafts, local products, Asian restaurants and tropical fruit juice stalls (mango, papaya, jackfruit). The pedestrian street facing the Esplanade lagoon is Cairns' social heart in the evening.

places.name: Cape Leveque

visits.text: Cape Leveque is the northernmost tip of the Dampier Peninsula, named in 1803 by French explorer Nicolas Baudin after his hydrographer Pierre Lévêque. In Bardi Jawi language, the place is called Kooljamanan. Pindan cliffs—the red-ochre sandstone characteristic of the Kimberley—plunge straight into turquoise water of exceptional clarity, creating the most striking color contrast on the Australian coast. The lighthouse, automated in 1986, was later purchased by the Aboriginal Development Commission for local Bardi Jawi communities.

Kooljaman is a 100% Indigenous-owned wilderness camp, managed by the Bardi Jawi communities of Djarindjin and Ardyaloon (One Arm Point). With no grid electricity or air conditioning—the camp runs on solar power and bore water—it offers safari tents, timber cabins, and beach shelters right on the sand. The camp has won multiple national Indigenous and ecotourism awards. The Dampier Peninsula is a dry zone: no alcohol is permitted.

activities.text: Snorkel the fringing reef at Eastern Beach, straight off Kooljaman's shore. Crystal-clear waters shelter corals, parrotfish, rays, and marine turtles regularly seen just meters from the beach. Swimming is safe on the eastern side—check with the camp about conditions and any hazards on the western shore.

Visit Ardyaloon (One Arm Point), 16 km south of Cape Leveque. The Bardi Jawi community runs a giant clam and sea turtle hatchery and offers guided tours. From here, spectacular views open across the Buccaneer Archipelago—a labyrinth of islands and islets subject to the continent's highest tides, up to 12 meters.

Watch the sunset from Western Beach at Kooljaman: red pindan cliffs ignite in the low-angled light as the Indian Ocean turns violet. This is the iconic Cape Leveque image—one of the most photographed scenes in the Kimberley. In season (June-November), humpback whales are sometimes visible offshore.

visits.text: Ningaloo Reef extends 260 km along the Coral Coast - Australia's longest fringing reef and one of the longest in the world. UNESCO World Heritage-listed in 2011, it's accessible directly from Coral Bay beach without needing a boat, less than 0.5 km from shore.

Ningaloo Reef shelters exceptional marine biodiversity: over 300 coral species, 500 fish species, 650 molluscs, 600 crustaceans and 155 sponges. The Baiyungu and Yinigudira traditional custodians have occupied this coast for over 32,000 years - shell artefacts among the oldest in the world have been discovered here.

activities.text: Pull on a mask and snorkel straight from Coral Bay beach. The reef is a few metres from shore: marine turtles, resident manta rays and schools of multicoloured fish are visible on beginner-level snorkels.

From March to July, Ningaloo hosts the world's largest documented aggregation of whale sharks (Rhincodon typus): 300 to 500 individuals drawn by the synchronized coral spawning. Swimming with the world's largest fish in these crystalline waters is the Coral Coast's flagship experience.

From July to October, migrating humpback whales cruise along Ningaloo. Regulated in-water whale encounters allow you to swim just metres from these 15-tonne cetaceans - a humbling, life-changing experience.

Cape Range National Park, 80 km north via Exmouth, unfolds red limestone gorges across the peninsula's spine. Turquoise Bay (snorkeling reef drift) and Mandu Mandu Gorge complete the terrestrial exploration of the UNESCO site.

days_plan.itin_title: Australia Grand Tour: Natural Wonders and Aboriginal Heritage

places.name: Darwin

visits.text: Darwin/Garramilla is the capital of the Northern Territory, built on Larrakia Country—the traditional custodians of the lands surrounding this harbour, five times larger than Sydney Harbour. Its Larrakia name, Garramilla, is pronounced garr-ah-mill-ah. The city was 80% destroyed on Christmas night 1974 by Cyclone Tracy—217 km/h winds recorded before the anemometer was ripped away, 66 dead, 36,000 residents evacuated in less than a week. Completely rebuilt under Australia's new cyclone codes, Darwin today counts over 30% overseas-born residents—Australia's most multicultural city by proportion.

The MAGNT (Museum and Art Gallery of the Northern Territory), perched on the cliffs overlooking the harbour, is the Territory's central cultural institution. Its permanent Cyclone Tracy exhibition (completely renovated in 2024 for the 50th anniversary) reconstructs the night of 24 December 1974 with archival sound recordings and survivor testimonies. Its Aboriginal art collection—one of Australia's most significant—spans bark paintings from Arnhem Land, Tiwi sculptures, and contemporary works. The emblematic piece: Sweetheart, the 5.1-metre saltwater crocodile captured in 1979 on the Finniss River after repeatedly attacking fishermen's outboard motors.

Crocosaurus Cove, right in the heart of Mitchell Street, houses Australia's largest collection of native reptiles. Its clear-water tanks showcase saltwater crocodiles (Crocodylus porosus) reaching 5 metres and 500 kg—the world's largest living reptiles. The 'Cage of Death', a transparent cage submerged directly into the main tank, allows face-to-face immersion with a crocodile without diving equipment. The Northern Territory's saltwater crocodiles have been a protected species since 1971; the local population is estimated at over 100,000 individuals.

Darwin's WWII Oil Storage Tunnels, hand-carved in 1942 following the first Japanese bombing on 19 February—the most devastating attack ever suffered by Australia, with 188 Japanese aircraft and more casualties than Pearl Harbor—form a network of 8 sandstone galleries 12 metres beneath the city. They were designed to store 8 million litres of fuel for the Allied fleet. Repurposed as an air-conditioned art gallery and memorial, they present period photographs and testimonies about the 188 raids Darwin endured between 1942 and 1943.

Parap Village Market (Saturday morning, 8am-2pm) is Darwin locals' weekly ritual—not the touristy Mindil Beach market, but the real neighbourhood market where Indonesian cooks, mango and papaya stalls, Larrakia crafts, and award-winning laksa have mingled for over forty years. Darwin laksa, spiced with lemongrass and galangal, is reputed to be Australia's finest.

Return to Darwin via Kakadu Highway then Stuart Highway (approximately 260 km, 2h50). En route, possible stop at Pine Creek (former gold rush village from the 19th century) and Nitmiluk National Park (Katherine Gorge) for those extending to Katherine—a 12 km limestone gorge carved by the Katherine River, navigable by kayak or cruise, on Jawoyn Country.

Free afternoon in Darwin. The city centre street art laneways—particularly around Smith Street Mall and the waterfront precinct—are the result of the annual Darwin Street Art Festival, which transformed the city's walls into a permanent gallery blending Larrakia, Tiwi, and international artists. The Parap neighbourhood houses several galleries of high-quality bark painting and contemporary Aboriginal art.

activities.text: Mindil Beach Sunset Market (Thursday and Sunday, May-October) is Darwin's defining social institution. Along 2 km of beach facing the Timor Sea, 200 food stalls blend Malaysian laksas, Balinese grills, Vietnamese spring rolls, and local barramundi over hot coals. The market opens at 5pm for the first sunsets at 7pm. Allow at least 2 hours—this is where Darwin reveals its simultaneous Asian and tropical identity.

Stroll the Darwin Waterfront Precinct, the redeveloped harbourfront at the foot of the CBD. The artificial Wave Lagoon (waves up to 1.5 m) and filtered-water swimming zone offer an alternative to beaches closed due to saltwater crocodiles. Dinner at Stokes Hill Wharf with harbour sunset views.

Adelaide River 'Jumping Crocodile' cruise, 60 km east of Darwin on the Arnhem Highway. Saltwater crocodiles leap out of the water to snatch suspended meat chunks from the boat deck—some individuals reach 5 metres and over 400 kg. The Adelaide River and its surrounding floodplains harbour the world's highest concentration of saltwater crocodiles. Recommended stop at the Window on the Wetlands Centre to observe the wetlands from the Arnhem Land escarpment.

Visit the George Brown Darwin Botanic Gardens (42 hectares), the Northern Territory's oldest botanical gardens, founded in 1879. The Matboerrma Walk explains Larrakia use of medicinal and food plants, with vernacular names indicated for each species. The tropical palm collection (over 400 species) is one of Australia's most comprehensive.

Farewell dinner at Hanuman Restaurant in Darwin, the city's benchmark address for Indo-Thai fusion cuisine. The menu draws on centuries-old trade relationships between northern Australia and Southeast Asia—Top End fish cooked according to Thai and Indian traditions with spices imported from Bali and Kerala. Reservations highly recommended in high season.

Return flight or connection to Perth, Sydney, or Melbourne from Darwin Airport, the Northern Territory's main hub. Daily direct flights to all Australian capital cities. Possible connections to the Kimberley (Derby, Broome) via regional flights with Airnorth.

places.name: Denmark

visits.text: Greens Pool, in William Bay National Park 14 km from Denmark, is one of Australia's most iconic beaches. Massive granite boulders form a natural reef that shelters a bay of near-still turquoise water—a lagoon carved from the Southern Ocean itself. Snorkellers spot Port Jackson sharks, stingrays, and schools of colourful fish in the crystal clarity. In summer, arrive before 9 am to secure parking.

A few hundred metres from Greens Pool, Elephant Rocks is a formation of rounded granite boulders that uncannily resemble a herd of elephants bathing in the water. These rocks, shaped by millions of years of erosion, overlook Elephant Cove and gaze out at the Southern Ocean. The site is believed to have been a sacred birthing place for Aboriginal peoples for centuries.

Ocean Beach is Denmark's big surf beach—facing due south, it receives Antarctic swells and draws surfers from up and down the coast. From nearby Lions Lookout, a 180° panorama embraces the entire sweep of Denmark Bay, Wilson Inlet, the forested hills, and on clear days, the Stirling Ranges to the north. It's one of the finest viewpoints on the Rainbow Coast.

Wilson Inlet is Western Australia's largest freshwater lagoon, separated from the Southern Ocean by a sandbar. Its calm shores attract 145 bird species—pelicans, ibis, royal spoonbills, and black swans in abundance—as well as dolphins that sometimes venture several kilometres upstream into the estuary.

activities.text: Snorkelling at Greens Pool in the sheltered lagoon waters. The submerged rocks harbour dense marine life: multicoloured fish, stingrays, and rock lobsters hiding in granite crevices. The water is clear and shallow near shore, ideal for beginners.

Coastal trail from Greens Pool to Elephant Rocks (1 km return) via the granite path linking the two sites along the headland. The track climbs slightly over the rocks—plunging views over both bays before descending to Elephant Cove.

Sunset from Lions Lookout above Ocean Beach. The panorama over Wilson Inlet, the sandbar, and the Southern Ocean is stunning in the golden hour. Denmark town is a 5-minute drive for dinner at one of its restaurants.

places.name: Derby

visits.text: Derby is the western gateway to the Gibb River Road. Established in 1883, it's the first settlement founded in the Kimberley — its wide boab-lined streets were deliberately designed wide enough for bullock teams to turn around.

The Boab Prison Tree, 7 km from Derby, is a 1,500-year-old baobab with a trunk circumference of 14.7 meters. It served as a holding cell for Aboriginal prisoners being transported to town. Now a protected cultural site.

Mowanjum Aboriginal Art and Cultural Centre showcases the creative work of the Worrorra, Ngarinyin and Wunumbal peoples. The paintings and sculptures feature Wandjina figures — creator spirits with enormous eyes and no mouths.

The Wharfingers House Museum chronicles Derby's maritime history. The adjacent Jetty, built in 1963, experiences Australia's highest tidal range: up to 12 meters of tidal movement makes mudflats appear and vanish with each cycle.

activities.text: Head to Derby Visitor Centre to check current track conditions, river crossing depths, and stock up on water, fuel and supplies before departure. This is your last full resupply point before Kununurra.
At sunset, walk out to Sculptures on the Marsh where metal structures up to 10 meters tall create striking silhouettes against the golden mudflats of King Sound.

places.name: Drysdale River

visits.text: Drysdale River Station is a family-run million-acre cattle station, 59 km north of the Gibb River Road / Kalumburu Road junction. It serves as the logistics base for travelers to Mitchell Plateau: caravan storage, fuel, restaurant, and scenic flights available.

Punamii-Uunpuu (Mitchell Falls): four tiers of cascades tumble over layers of sandstone across a tropical plateau held sacred by the Wunambal Gaambera people. The sheer gorge walls shelter some of the Kimberley's oldest rock art sites — Wandjina and Gwion Gwion (Bradshaw) paintings.

activities.text: Hike to Mitchell Falls (8.6 km return, 3–4h). The trail crosses Mertens Creek, passes Little Mertens Falls and Big Mertens Falls — two breathtaking intermediate cascades — before reaching the main falls. Swimming possible in pools above the falls.
Optionally, combine the hike with a helicopter return flight: lift off from the falls lookout and soar over the four-tiered cascade, gorges, and Kimberley coastline.
En route, stop at the King Edward River crossing — a clear waterway lined with pandanus, perfect for cooling off and birdwatching.

days_plan.itin_title: Kimberley Crossing Australia: Wild Outback Track Adventure

map_keywords: Drysdale River Station Kalumburu Road Kimberley

visits.text: The Port of Echuca, built in 1865, was at its peak in the 1870s the largest inland port in Australia. Its red gum wharf stretched 332 metres and welcomed more than 240 paddle steamers a year, ferrying wool, wheat and timber downriver to Melbourne.

The Discovery Centre traces the golden age of river trade on the Murray. Interactive exhibits, working steam engines and boatmen's stories bring to life the pioneers who transported cargo through Australia's inland waterways.

The Murray - the world's third-longest navigable river after the Nile and the Amazon - meanders over 2,000 km from the foothills of Kosciuszko to the Southern Ocean. The Yorta Yorta Aboriginal people have lived along its banks for more than 40,000 years.

activities.text: Board an authentic paddle steamer for a one-hour cruise - PS Canberra (built 1913), PS Pevensey or PS Emmylou. The captain explains the steam workings and recounts tales from the port's heyday as you chug past river red gum forests.

The Silo Art Trail links several grain silos painted by Australian artists between Echuca and Sea Lake - the country's largest outdoor gallery. A few of the most spectacular murals are within an hour's drive from town.

Walk or cycle the Murray River banks to spot black swans, Australian pelicans and royal herons among the river red gum forests. These towering eucalypts supplied the timber for the port's wharves in the 19th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Australia Grand Tour: Natural Wonders and Aboriginal Heritage

places.name: El Questro

visits.text: El Questro Wilderness Park covers 700,000 acres of the East Kimberley: sandstone gorges, thermal springs, riverine forests, and vast wild expanses. A former cattle station opened to tourism in 1991, the park offers an unmatched diversity of experiences on the Gibb River Road.

Emma Gorge is framed by the sandstone cliffs of the Cockburn Range. After an hour's hike up a pandanus-lined creek, a 65-meter waterfall plunges into a turquoise pool. A warm spring trickles discreetly from the right wall — seek it out after your swim.
Zebedee Springs: a series of thermal pools at 32°C linked together at the base of a gorge filled with prehistoric Livistona palms. Open to the public 7am–12pm only. A 400-meter walk from the carpark.

activities.text: Embark on the Chamberlain Gorge cruise (approximately 3 km accessible only by boat). Guides point out saltwater crocodiles lurking beneath the walls, rock wallabies, and archerfish that spit jets of water to knock down insects up to 3 meters high.

Drive up to Branco's Lookout late afternoon via the 17-km 4WD track: sunset panorama across the Elgee Cliffs and the Chamberlain River meanders from the lookout named after a former Wyndham shire president.
Fish for barramundi on the Pentecost or Chamberlain Rivers. Guides offer 4WD trips to exclusive spots where threadfin salmon and catfish also hide.

places.name: Gold Coast

visits.text: Gold Coast is a beachfront metropolis of 600,000 where residential towers directly line 70 km of surf beaches. Surfers Paradise, with its dense skyline and 24/7 urban beach, is the Gold Coast showcase—a striking contrast with the hinterland rainforests 40 minutes away. SkyPoint Observation Deck on Q1 tower's 77th floor (322 m, 2005) offers panoramic views to Cape Byron.

Springbrook National Park, 40 km into the Gold Coast hinterland, preserves UNESCO-listed subtropical rainforest (Gondwana Rainforests of Australia). Natural Bridge is a 54-million-year-old basalt arch crossed by a waterfall—a colony of glow worms (Arachnocampa flava) inhabits the cave below, visible on guided night visits.

activities.text: Hot air balloon flight at sunrise over Tamborine Mountain vineyards and hinterland forests (local operators from Surfers Paradise). Land alternative: hike the Rainforest Circuit at Springbrook (2.5 km, 1h, easy) through temperate forest with plunging Gold Coast and Pacific views from Best of All Lookout.

places.name: Hervey Bay

visits.text: K'gari (formerly Fraser Island), officially renamed in 2023 to its traditional Butchulla name, is the world's largest sand island at 122 km long. UNESCO-listed since 1992, it's globally unique for the combination of its sand dunes—reaching 244 m height—its rainforests growing directly on sand, and its 100+ freshwater lakes. It also holds the world's largest unconfined aquifer on a sand island. K'gari's dingoes are among Australia's most genetically pure, isolated from crossbreeding with domestic dogs.

Lake McKenzie (Boorangoora in Butchulla language) is K'gari's jewel: a perched dune lake, fed exclusively by rainwater filtered through almost pure silica sand. Its absolutely clear waters, surrounded by blindingly white beach, reach 23°C in summer. Its surface covers 150 hectares at 7 meters maximum depth. It's accessible only by 4WD track from Eurong (40 min)—national vehicle permit required.

75 Mile Beach is the official sand highway running K'gari's entire east coast for 120 km—with road signs, 80 km/h speed limits and even an airstrip. Access is tide-dependent (passage impossible at high tide). Features include the SS Maheno wreck (1935), a converted hospital ship, rusted and sand-buried, and Indian Head, a rocky cape from which rays, sharks and turtles are visible in the clear waters below.

activities.text: K'gari 4WD exploration: rental from Hervey Bay or Rainbow Beach (required—no conventional vehicles permitted on island). National park permit required in addition to vehicle permit. Guided tours from Hervey Bay (full day) include Lake McKenzie, 75 Mile Beach, Maheno wreck, Eli Creek (freshwater stream where you float on the current) and Indian Head.

places.name: Home Valley

visits.text: Ellenbrae Station, 64 km past the Kalumburu junction, is a legendary Gibb River Road stopover. The homemade scones served on the veranda have passed through generations — a bittersweet pause before the final long stretch.

Home Valley Station sprawls across 1.4 million acres on the banks of the Pentecost River, beneath the gaze of the Cockburn Ranges. The station is owned by the Indigenous Land Corporation and operates as a training academy for Aboriginal communities of the East Kimberley.

activities.text: The Pentecost River crossing is one of the most photographed scenes in the Australian outback: your 4WD rolls into brick-red water with the Cockburn Ranges escarpments as backdrop. Check depth before entering — saltwater crocodiles frequent the banks.

The Cockburn Ranges lookout, signposted on the Gibb River Road, offers sweeping views across the western face of the red sandstone escarpments and the confluence of the Durack and Pentecost Rivers. Stunning at sunset.
Embark on a horseback riding tour from Home Valley Station. Guides lead visitors across the pastures and down to the banks of the Pentecost River — an authentic immersion in Kimberley station life.
Bindoola Falls, accessible on foot from the Gibb River Road, offers a glimpse of what the gorge would look like in full wet season. During the dry, the residual pools are perfect for swimming.

days_plan.itin_title: Kimberley Crossing Australia: Wild Outback Track Adventure

map_keywords: Home Valley Station Gibb River Road

places.name: Hunter Valley

visits.text: Hunter Valley is Australia's oldest wine region: the first vines were planted in 1820 by James Busby, considered the father of Australian viticulture. The region produces Australia's most renowned Semillons—a white grape that ages exceptionally well in the valley's volcanic soil—along with internationally recognized Shiraz and Chardonnay. Over 150 estates welcome visitors, concentrated around Pokolbin village.

Historic grand estates like Tyrrell's Wines (founded 1858, the region's oldest winery) and Brokenwood (founded 1970) offer guided tastings with terroir explanations. The Buschenschenken-style tasting rooms allow comparison of young and aged Semillons—the style difference is striking—accompanied by local cheese and charcuterie platters.

activities.text: Hot air balloon flight at sunrise over the vineyards with Balloon Aloft (5:30am departure). The Hunter Valley sky at daybreak, with morning mists between vine rows and the Brokenback Ranges as backdrop, offers one of Australia's most spectacular panoramas. 1-hour flight + champagne on landing.

Hunter Valley Gardens at Pokolbin is a 10-hectare themed garden park (Chinese garden, English garden, rose garden, Tuscan garden)—a peaceful late-afternoon visit before dinner at one of the region's many restaurants.

places.name: Jabiru

visits.text: Kakadu National Park (1,980,400 hectares—half the size of Switzerland) is Australia's largest national park and one of the world's largest. Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in three stages (1981, 1987, 1992) for its simultaneous natural and cultural values—one of the few sites worldwide listed for both criteria—it is co-managed by the Bininj/Mungguy, whose ancestors have inhabited this country for over 60,000 years. More than 5,000 rock art sites have been recorded. The fauna: 68 mammal species, 290 bird species (one-third of Australia's avifauna), 120 reptiles, 26 frogs.

Ubirr is one of Kakadu's two major rock art galleries, 40 km north of Jabiru. Its 1 km circuit traverses several rock shelters whose walls layer paintings spanning over 20,000 years of continuous occupation—from Pleistocene 'dynamic figures' style through 'X-ray' representations revealing animals' internal anatomy to depictions of first European contact. The galleries of Mimi figures—stick-thin spirits who run so fast the wind breaks them—are among the oldest visual records in human history. Access is conditional during the wet season (flooding).

Nadab Lookout, atop the Ubirr circuit (250-metre moderate climb from the gallery), offers 360° panoramic views over the East Alligator River floodplains and Arnhem Land escarpments. This is Kakadu's most photographed viewpoint—especially at sunset when the wetlands ignite in slanting light. The dry season concentrates thousands of magpie geese, egrets, and jabirus around residual water bodies.

Nourlangie (Burrunggui in Bininj language), 19 km south of Jabiru, is Kakadu's second major painted rock complex. The 1.5 km circuit (easy, 45 min) follows the imposing red sandstone cliff to reach Anbangbang gallery—the park's largest and most spectacular—where Namarrgon, the Lightning Man, is depicted in full, with stone axes bound to his elbows and knees that generate the wet season's lightning. The Gunwarddehwardde lookout, perched high, overlooks the Arnhem Land escarpments.

Anbangbang Billabong, adjacent to the Nourlangie site, is a water body framed by giant waterlilies (Nymphaea violacea) and pandanus, accessible via a 2.5 km trail around the lake. Freshwater and saltwater crocodiles are regularly observed from the boardwalks. In the dry season, reef egrets, cormorants, and azure kingfishers occupy every dead branch above the water.

Jim Jim Falls (Barrkmalam), 60 km south of Jabiru via a 10 km 4WD track (May-Oct only), is Kakadu's iconic waterfall. In the wet season, a single cascade plunges 215 metres into the gorge. In the dry season, when flow diminishes, a crystal-clear water body ringed by 215-metre vertical cliffs forms one of Australia's most spectacular swimming holes. The 1 km walk from the car park traverses fallen sandstone boulders. Saltwater crocodile presence is verified by rangers before swimming opens.

Twin Falls, 10 km from Jim Jim via 4WD track then inflatable boat crossing (operator on-site), is Kakadu's most remote and most rewarding site. Twin cascades drop 150 metres into a red sandstone canyon accessible only by water. The white sand beach at the gorge's end, framed by tropical ferns and strangler figs, is accessible only by small boat (20 min from car park). The natural cathedral atmosphere alone justifies the journey.

activities.text: Stop at Bowali Visitor Centre, 5 km from Jabiru, to purchase your Kakadu pass and check road conditions and ranger schedules. The centre presents a comprehensive introduction to Bininj/Mungguy culture—the six seasons of the calendar (Kunumeleng, Kudjewk, Banggerreng, Yegge, Wurrgeng, Gurrung), clan line meanings, country protocols—and helps orient visits according to currently accessible zones.

Birdwatching at Mamukala Wetlands Bird Hide, 29 km west of Jabiru on the Arnhem Highway. In late dry season, up to 40,000 magpie geese (Anseranas semipalmata) concentrate on the last remaining billabongs, forming one of Australia's largest bird gatherings. The teakwood observation hide allows approach to within 50 metres.

Yellow Water (Ngurrungurrudjba) sunset cruise from the Cooinda jetty, 50 km south of Jabiru. Yellow Water Billabong is the wet heart of Kakadu—its slow-moving brackish waters concentrate saltwater crocodiles (sometimes 5 metres and more, visible within 5 metres of the boat), jacanas walking on waterlilies, ospreys perched on termite mounds, and hundreds of grey herons in dead trees at dusk. The evening cruise (1.5 hours) is unanimously considered Kakadu's best experience.

Visit the Warradjan Aboriginal Cultural Centre at Cooinda, one of two park cultural centres (with Bowali). Circular in the shape of a freshwater crocodile (warradjan in Bininj language), it presents Bininj/Mungguy cosmology—the Emu Dreaming, Rainbow Serpent, Namarrgon and Warramurrungundji the Earth Mother—through ceremonial objects, oral tradition recordings, and bark painting artworks on eucalyptus bark.

For those without 4WD, Maguk (Barramundi Gorge)—accessible via sealed road + 3 km walk—offers a similar experience in smaller scale: sandstone gorge framed by pandanus, 15-metre waterfall into a deep transparent pool. It's one of southern Kakadu's best-kept secrets.

Late afternoon: return toward Jabiru with stop at Gunlom Falls (Udurrwudurr), 170 km south of Jabiru via Kakadu Highway (4WD recommended). The natural pool atop the falls—perched on the escarpment edge—is considered Australia's finest natural 'infinity pool', with views over southern Kakadu's plains. The 2.5 km climb is steep (200 m elevation gain) but the site justifies the effort.

places.name: Jervis Bay

visits.text: The Grand Pacific Drive is a 140 km coastal route between Sydney and Wollongong, punctuated by Pacific Ocean viewpoints. Its main attraction is Sea Cliff Bridge (2005)—a 665-meter suspended steel arch built into the cliff face above the ocean, allowing the road to bypass inaccessible cliffs. Successive lookouts over the Illawarra region beaches offer some of the NSW coast's finest views.

Hyams Beach, in Jervis Bay Territory, is celebrated for having the world's whitest beach sand according to Guinness World Records—almost pure silica of blinding whiteness. The bay itself, with exceptionally clear waters, shelters a permanent colony of bottlenose dolphins (approximately 80 individuals) and forms a protected marine area within Booderee National Park.

activities.text: Dolphin watching from the beach or on boat excursions from Huskisson (Jervis Bay's main town). Sunset cruises allow observation of dolphins hunting in the bay—local operators guarantee sightings in most cases. Sea kayaks are available for rent to explore the coves of Booderee National Park.

Snorkeling at Murrays Beach within Booderee National Park. Calm, crystal-clear waters, tropical and temperate fish, sea turtles. One of the NSW coast's best snorkeling beaches, freely accessible from a marked parking area.

places.name: Kakadu

visits.text: Ubirr is a rock outcrop on the banks of the Nadab floodplain in the East Alligator region. Its rock galleries document 20,000 years of continuous human occupation: X-ray style paintings showing skeletons of barramundi and turtles, Mimi spirits with thread-like bodies, and a depiction of the thylacine (Tasmanian tiger) - a species extinct from the mainland for over 2,000 years.

Burrunggui (Nourlangie Rock) is a sandstone promontory of the Arnhem Land escarpment. Its shelters contain paintings layered over millennia: creator ancestors, Namarrgon the 'Lightning Man', and depictions of the first European buffalo that arrived in the 1880s. More than 200,000 visitors come here annually.

Kakadu covers 20,000 km² (the size of Slovenia), UNESCO World Heritage-listed since 1981 for both natural AND cultural values simultaneously - a rare dual inscription. The park contains over 5,000 rock art sites, some estimated at more than 30,000 years old.

activities.text: Take the Ubirr panoramic trail to the Nadab lookout (500 m, 20 min). At sunset, the view over the floodplains, the Arnhem Land escarpment and swirling flocks of birds is among Australia's most breathtaking.

A dawn cruise on Yellow Water Billabong offers sightings of wild saltwater crocodiles, sea eagles, jabiru storks and Asian otters in the rice-grass of the floodplain - an untamed wetland teeming with life.

Visit the Warradjan Aboriginal Cultural Centre near Cooinda: this museum shaped like a pig-nosed turtle explains the six seasons of the Bininj/Mungguy calendar, Dreaming rituals and the links between clans and their Country.

In the dry season (May-October), reach Jim Jim Falls via dirt track (4WD recommended, 60 km from Arnhem Highway). The falls plunge 150 m from the escarpment into a natural plunge pool surrounded by red sandstone walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Australia Grand Tour: Natural Wonders and Aboriginal Heritage

map_keywords: Ubirr rock art Kakadu National Park

places.name: Kununurra

visits.text: Kununurra, meaning 'meeting of big waters' in the Miriwoong language, is the gateway to the East Kimberley. Founded in 1963 for the Ord River Scheme, the town is surrounded by vast tropical crop fields irrigated by Lake Argyle.

Mirima National Park (Hidden Valley), 2 km from town center, concentrates millions-of-years-old red sandstone formations — a miniature Bungle Bungles within walking distance. Four trails reveal boabs clinging to cliffs, grinding stones, and Aboriginal petroglyphs.

Lake Argyle, 70 km from Kununurra, is Australia's largest artificial reservoir: 1,000 km² in the dry season (18 times the area of Sydney Harbour). It hosts over a third of Australia's bird species and shelters 25,000 freshwater crocodiles.

Wyndham, 100 km from Kununurra, is Western Australia's northernmost town. The Five Rivers Lookout overlooks the confluence of the Ord, Pentecost, King, Forrest and Durack Rivers flowing into Cambridge Gulf — a panorama of rare scale.

activities.text: As an optional excursion (250 km return), Purnululu National Park is home to the Bungle Bungles: 350 beehive towers of black-and-orange banded sandstone, UNESCO World Heritage listed. The gorges of Cathedral Gorge and Echidna Chasm look like nothing else on Earth. Scenic flights available from Kununurra.

Sunset cruise on Lake Argyle: pink granite rocks emerge from the water, fishing eagles soar overhead, and hundreds of freshwater crocodiles glide onto the banks. Swim in the resort's infinity pool in the evening.
Drive up to Kelly's Knob Lookout at sunset — a short climb from the carpark delivers 360° views over town, the Ord Valley, irrigated fields, and the Carr Boyd Ranges bathed in golden light.

Visit Waringarri Aboriginal Arts to meet Miriwoong artists and discover paintings, sculptures, and objects from the traditional people of the East Kimberley. Cultural tours guided by Elders are sometimes available at sunset in Mirima National Park.

places.name: Litchfield

visits.text: Litchfield National Park (1,500 km², 120 km southwest of Darwin) is the Top End locals' beloved park—less crowded than Kakadu but more accessible for swimming. Sacred country of the Koongurrukun, Mak Mak Marranunggu, Werat, and Warray, its ancestral spirits shaped the sandstone cliffs of the Tabletop Range, monsoon forests, and permanent spring-fed rivers. The magnetic termite mounds—up to 2 metres tall, oriented precisely north-south to regulate temperature—and cathedral termite mounds—up to 8 metres—that dot the savannas were built over decades by grass-eating termites of the Termitidae family.

Wangi Falls, on the park's western edge, is Litchfield's most popular and accessible waterfall. Its twin cascades tumble in successive tiers from 50 metres into a 100-metre-wide swimming hole surrounded by dense monsoon forest. The 3 km loop trail climbs above the falls to the lookout platform over the ironwood forests before descending through gallery forest. In the dry season, the pool is declared safe after ranger crocodile checks.

Florence Falls, 15 km east of Wangi, is the locals' favourite for its water quality and lower crowds. Accessible from the car park via 160 steps down to the pool or via a viewing platform (3-minute walk), it plunges into a swimming hole ringed by monsoon vegetation. The Florence Creek walk (3.2 km) links Florence Falls to Buley Rockhole through gallery forest and open woodlands—one of the park's finest trails.

activities.text: Swim at Buley Rockhole—a series of natural cascading pools carved into red sandstone by Florence Creek. Shallower and less crowded than Wangi or Florence Falls, its natural rock pools tumble downstream in gentle rapids between smooth boulders. Perfect for an extended break after the Florence Creek walk (natural arrival point if you walk from Florence Falls).

Stop at the magnetic termite mound viewing area on the park's main road. The boardwalk winds through a field of several hundred mounds oriented precisely north-south—a remarkable thermal adaptation the termites likely achieve by detecting Earth's magnetic field. Nearby, the cathedral termite mounds (different species, grass-eating) reach 8 metres and can be over 100 years old.

places.name: Manning Gorge

visits.text: Manning Gorge sits 7 km from Mt Barnett Roadhouse, deep within Mt Barnett Station, owned by the Kupungarri Aboriginal community since 1988. The campground, right on the sandy bank of the Manning River, serves as the starting point for the hike to the upper falls.

activities.text: To reach the Upper Manning Falls, cross the Manning River using the provided dinghy — or the blue floating drums for backpacks. The 6 km return hike (1.5–2h each way) crosses open savanna and sandstone country before reaching the Gibb River Road's largest waterfall. Early season, water pours across the full width of the rock shelf.
The Manning River at the campground offers a perfect sandy beach for swimming, relaxing and using the rope swing. Stay in the river to spot freshwater turtles and tropical fish.
En route from Bell Gorge, detour to Galvans Gorge (1 km off-track): a horseshoe pool shaded by trees with Aboriginal rock art, a centuries-old boab, and the famous rope swing.

Mt Barnett Roadhouse Gibb River Road

places.name: Margaret River

visits.text: Cape Naturaliste Lighthouse, commissioned in 1904, stands sentinel at the northern tip of the Leeuwin-Naturaliste Peninsula, perched 100 metres above the Indian Ocean. Its 59 steps lead to a 360° viewing platform overlooking Geographe Bay and the jagged cliffs of the national park. It's one of the few operational lighthouses still standing in Australia. Between September and December, humpback and southern right whales cruise past the headland—the lighthouse serves as one of the region's premier whale-watching vantage points.

Canal Rocks is a spectacular granite formation where millennia of Indian Ocean erosion have carved natural channels through massive boulders. The sea surges through these rocky passages in seething torrents. A steel footbridge spans the formation, allowing visitors to venture right into the heart of the rocks without risk. The site is particularly dramatic during heavy swells, when spray explodes between the granite walls.

Injidup Natural Spa is a granite rock pool carved by nature itself, just minutes from Yallingup in Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park. Indian Ocean swells funnel through a fissure in the rock and fill the basin with swirling, churning water—a natural jacuzzi courtesy of the sea. Sacred to the Wadandi people, the saltwater custodians who have inhabited these lands for millennia. Swimming is not recommended during heavy swells; low tide on calm days offers the safest conditions.

Sugarloaf Rock, visible from the Cape Naturaliste road, is an isolated granite monolith encircled by white foam. Ranked among the most photographed coastal formations in southwest Australia, it sits below the trails of the Cape-to-Cape Track. The view is most striking at sunrise, when the pink rock catches the first light.

Jewel Cave is Western Australia's largest show cave, discovered in 1957 by a local timber worker named Ron Sinkinson. Its three enormous chambers—Jewel, Reflection, and Empress—are adorned with stalactites, stalagmites, helictites, and shawls sculpted over millennia from aeolian limestone. The showpiece is one of the world's longest straw stalactites, measuring 5.4 metres. Thylacine (Tasmanian tiger) fossils were found deep in the cave, trapped after a fatal fall.

Cape Leeuwin Lighthouse, commissioned on 10 December 1896, is mainland Australia's tallest lighthouse, its 39-metre tower built from local limestone. It marks the continent's most southwestern point—where the Indian and Southern Oceans collide in a turbulent embrace. Along with the Cape of Good Hope and Cape Horn, it's one of the three great capes that mariners must round during a circumnavigation. The lighthouse remains operational today. It's named after a Dutch ship, the Leeuwin ('Lioness'), which charted this coast in 1622.

Gnarabup Beach, 3 km from Margaret River town, is a long stretch of white sand protected by offshore reefs—far gentler than the neighbouring surf breaks. The rocks bordering the bay to the south form a chaotic jumble of erosion-sculpted formations. It's the locals' favourite spot for family swimming.

activities.text: Walk the Cape Naturaliste Circuit Loop (3.8 km, 1.5 hours return) from the lighthouse car park. The trail follows wooden boardwalks clinging to cliff edges, passes through Indian Ocean lookouts, and joins the Sugarloaf Rock Trail before looping back to the lighthouse. Breathtaking views of the two oceans colliding offshore.

Head down to Meelup Bay beach at dusk. Sheltered by the Cape Naturaliste headland, its intensely turquoise waters are among the calmest on the coast—perfect for a late-afternoon swim before heading into Margaret River town.

Guided climb to the top of Cape Leeuwin Lighthouse (176 steps) to hear keepers' tales and gaze out at both oceans from the lantern room 39 metres high. The Lightkeepers' Museum, housed in one of the original cottages, traces the lives of the families who've tended this isolated cape since 1896.

In the afternoon, tour the cellar doors of the Margaret River Wine Region along Caves Road. The region is renowned for exceptional Cabernet Sauvignon and Chardonnay—it represents just 3% of Australian wine production but 20% of premium award-winning wines. Most estates offer free or paid tastings on weekdays; some open their cellars by appointment.
End the day at Gnarabup Beach for sunset. The golden evening light on the reefs and white sand is one of the coast's most photogenic scenes. Dolphins are often spotted in the bay late in the day.

places.name: Melbourne

visits.text: Melbourne, capital of Victoria, is one of Australia's most dynamic cities. Its network of laneways threading through the CBD dates back to the 1837 Hoddle Grid—originally designed as service alleys, these cobbled arteries transformed from the 1990s onward into open-air galleries. Hosier Lane, officially opened as a street art gallery in 1998, stretches opposite Federation Square: its walls change constantly, with works by artists from around the globe including pieces by Banksy that appeared until 2010.

Federation Square, inaugurated in October 2002 above the Flinders Street railway lines, is Melbourne's civic heart. This deconstructivist complex designed by LAB Architecture houses the Australian Centre for the Moving Image (ACMI) and the Ian Potter Centre: NGV Australia. The vast open plaza hosts public gatherings, sports screenings, and festivals throughout the year.

The Royal Arcade, opened in 1869, is Australia's oldest continuously operating shopping arcade. Built in Italianate style with glass domes and black-and-white mosaic floors, it houses the legendary statues of Gog and Magog that chime the hours. A few steps away, the Block Arcade (1892) offers the same Victorian elegance with its stained glass and artisan boutiques.

activities.text: Little penguin viewing at St Kilda Pier at dusk. Each evening, a colony of little penguins (Eudyptula minor) returns to nest beneath the pier after a day fishing at sea. It's one of the world's few urban penguin colonies—free access, but flash photography is prohibited to avoid disturbing the birds.

Walk through St Kilda from the Esplanade to the beach. This bohemian seaside suburb hosts the famous Sunday artisan market on the Upper Esplanade (Sundays, 9am–4pm) with over 200 arts and crafts vendors.

places.name: Mission Beach

visits.text: Mission Beach is a tropical village of 2,500, squeezed between the Wet Tropics UNESCO rainforest and the Pacific Ocean. The 14 km of deserted beaches are directly bordered by forest canopy—here the rainforest literally touches the sand. It's the natural habitat of the southern cassowary (Casuarius casuarius), the world's third-largest bird: trail encounters are possible but require keeping distance—males can be aggressive.

Mission Beach is considered Australia's best skydiving location: tandem jumps from 4,200 m offer simultaneous views of rainforest to the west and the Great Barrier Reef (its reefs visible to the naked eye in turquoise waters) to the east—a view impossible from any other point on the Australian coast.

activities.text: Snorkeling excursion to Dunk Island (15 min ferry), a national park island covered in rainforest with fringing reef in excellent condition around the island. Mission Beach reef is part of the central Great Barrier Reef sector, less frequented than Cairns and with reef in better condition. Sea kayaks available for rent on Main Beach.

visits.text: Mornington Wildlife Sanctuary is one of Australia's largest private non-government reserves: 565,000 hectares managed by Australian Wildlife Conservancy (AWC), spanning four river systems including the Fitzroy River. The camp protects key populations of Gouldian Finch, Purple-crowned Fairy-wren, and Northern Quoll.
Dimond Gorge is the sanctuary's jewel: the Fitzroy River flows between red sandstone walls that plunge straight into the water. The gorge is accessible only by canoe from the camp.
Sir John Gorge, a few kilometers from camp, offers a crystal-clear natural pool set against escarpments and savanna. Perfect for a sunset swim with views of the colorful cliffs.

activities.text: Rent a canoe from camp and paddle to Dimond Gorge: the sandstone walls frame a calm water corridor you explore at your own pace. A unique Kimberley experience.
Join the morning guided birding tour led by AWC ecologists to secret spots for observing Gouldian Finches, kingfishers and raptors. One of the Kimberley's finest birdwatching experiences.
The camp offers several self-guided walks through savanna and along gorge edges. In the evening, attend the free presentation by AWC ecologists on Kimberley wildlife conservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Kimberley Crossing Australia: Wild Outback Track Adventure

places.name: Mount Barker

visits.text: The Castle Rock Granite Skywalk in Porongurup National Park (15 minutes from Mount Barker) is a steel-mesh structure bolted directly into granite, perched atop a chaos of rocks 1.2 billion years old—Australia's oldest mountain range. The Porongurup Range stretches 12 km and harbours one of the highest concentrations of endemic plants in southwest Australia. On clear days, the panorama from the Skywalk embraces the Stirling Ranges to the north and the distant Southern Ocean.

Bluff Knoll (Pualaar Miial—'great many-faced hill'), the 1,095-metre summit in Stirling Range National Park, is the highest point in the southern half of Western Australia. Its sheer cliffs, winter snow gullies, and 1,500 plant species (many found nowhere else) make it one of the continent's most singular peaks. The Noongar name for the range, Koi Kyenunu-ruff, means 'mist rolling around the mountains'—a phenomenon visible almost daily from the plains.

activities.text: Ascent to Castle Rock Granite Skywalk (4.4 km return, 2h). The trail passes through jarrah and karri forest before climbing over granite boulders via a series of steel ladders and walkways anchored into the rock. The Skywalk itself—a mesh-floored platform over a sheer drop—offers a unique 360° panorama of the Porongurup Range.

Hike Bluff Knoll (6.3 km return, 637 m elevation gain). The well-marked stepped trail climbs through schist and quartzite cliffs to the 1,095 m summit—the 360° views over the plains, the Porongurup, and on exceptional days, the coast 100 km away, are worth the effort. Bring a warm jacket even in summer: the summit experiences WA's only alpine microclimate.

Wine tasting in the Mount Barker Wine Region late in the day. This cool-climate wine zone produces excellent Riesling, Pinot Noir, and Cabernet Sauvignon. Estates are scattered in the hills around town—some offer cheese and charcuterie boards paired with wine flights.

places.name: Mount Hart

visits.text: Mount Hart Wilderness Lodge occupies a former cattle station deep in Wunaamin Conservation Park. The property spans nearly a million acres across the King Leopold Ranges. The 50-km access track winds through green valleys and spectacular geological formations.
Matthew Gorge, a few kilometers from the lodge, is an intimate gorge where black boulders frame dozens of pools, rapids and small cascades linked together. Perfect for peaceful aquatic exploration.

activities.text: Swim at Barker Pool, a wide crystal-clear section of the Barker River that flows past the campground — the best swim of the day before the afternoon heat sets in.
Follow the Annie Creek trail, a flat shaded path along a sheltered creek. The lodge gardens attract Gouldian Finches, kingfishers and cockatoos — an ideal birdwatching stroll at dawn.
Late afternoon, drive up Sunset Hill via a short 4WD track for panoramic views across the Range valleys at sunset — one of the most silent and intense spectacles of the entire route.

places.name: Murwillumbah

visits.text: Wollumbin (formerly Mount Warning) is a 1,156-meter volcanic neck, remnant of the southern hemisphere's largest volcanic shield's eruptive center. Twenty million years ago, this giant volcano of 100 km diameter collapsed to form the Tweed Caldera—what we now call Tweed Valley. Wollumbin means in Bundjalung language 'cloud catcher' or 'warrior chief.' Each morning, it's the first point on the Australian mainland touched by sunrise.

Important cultural note: since August 2024, summit ascent of Wollumbin has been closed by Bundjalung Traditional Custodians out of respect for the site's sacred character—only the trail to the lower foothills (Lyrebird Track, 1.5 km one-way, 45 min) remains open. The decision respects Aboriginal cultural sovereignty over their sacred sites. The rainforest of the foothills, UNESCO-listed as part of Gondwana Rainforests of Australia, remains fully accessible.

activities.text: Rainforest hiking in Tweed Valley from trails in Wollumbin National Park. Gondwanan vegetation—tree ferns, strangler figs, Bangalow palms—forms a dense canopy accessible from several entrances in Tweed Valley. Waterfalls like Crystal Creek Falls and Minyon Falls (59 m) complete the excursion.

places.name: Noosa

visits.text: Noosa Heads is an upscale Queensland resort town at the Noosa River mouth, known for its main beach with long swells perfectly suited to longboarding and its preserved natural setting. Hastings Street, the car-free main strip, concentrates designer boutiques, award-winning restaurants and specialty cafés in the relaxed atmosphere that built Noosa's lifestyle reputation.

Noosa Heads National Park, walkable from Main Beach, is one of the few Australian national parks enclosed within an urban area. Its 11 km coastal trail traverses rocky headlands with Pacific Ocean views and leads to wild coves accessible only on foot. Wild koalas are frequently observed in the park's eucalyptus trees, especially in the morning.

activities.text: Kayaking in the Noosa Everglades from Noosa River—a network of shallow channels and lakes crossing paperbark forest (Melaleuca), nicknamed the Australian Everglades. Guided excursions depart from Lake Cootharaba (45 min from Noosa by car) and traverse uniquely strange vegetation landscape. Waterbird observation (jabiru, sacred ibis, egrets) virtually guaranteed.

places.name: Palm Cove

visits.text: Palm Cove is a miniature seaside village - 2,450 residents - tucked 27 km north of Cairns. Its esplanade is lined with an avenue of centuries-old melaleucas (over 500 years), their roots plunging into the sand like natural sculptures.

Port Douglas, 40 km further north, is a fishing village turned tropical resort town. Its main street, Macrossan Street, packs restaurants and boutiques two steps from Four Mile Beach - a 6-km sweep of sand facing the Coral Sea.

activities.text: Tackle the first section of the Wangetti Trail from Palm Cove Jetty. This 7.8 km route to Ellis Beach (Grade 3, about 3h one-way) crosses Macalister Range National Park along ancient Djabugay pathways. Carry at least 3 litres of water.
From Palm Cove or Port Douglas, tour operators offer half-day snorkeling trips to the outer Great Barrier Reef. Green turtles, parrotfish and rays are regularly spotted in these pristine waters.

Drive the Captain Cook Highway between Palm Cove and Port Douglas at sunset: the road snakes between the forested escarpments of the Macalister Range and the Coral Sea, with plunging views over Double Island and Trinity Beach.

places.name: Pemberton

visits.text: The Gloucester Tree is a giant karri (Eucalyptus diversicolor) standing 61 metres tall, 2 kilometres from Pemberton in Gloucester National Park. Selected in 1947 as a fire lookout tower, it's the second-tallest lookout tree in the world. Its 153 steel spikes, hammered in a spiral pattern, once allowed forest rangers to climb without harnesses to a platform 53 metres above ground to watch for smoke. The tree is currently closed for climbing due to platform renovation works; reopening is expected soon.

The karri forests encircling Pemberton are the world's third-tallest hardwood forests—karris can exceed 90 metres. Their smooth trunks shift colour with age, from pale pink to orange and silvery white, creating light-dappled cathedrals beneath the canopy. The Karri Forest Explorer Drive is an 86 km signposted circuit through five national parks.

Beedelup Falls, in Greater Beedelup National Park 20 minutes from Pemberton, cascade over granite slabs before joining Beedelup Brook. The 25-metre suspension bridge spanning the base of the falls is an attraction in itself—it sways underfoot. Further along the trail, a hollow karri allows walkers to pass right through its trunk.

activities.text: Walk to the Gloucester Tree (6 km return from Pemberton town) following the blue trail markers through karri forest. Even closed to climbing, the imposing trunk and spiral of steel spikes make an irresistible photographic subject.

Afternoon wine tasting in the Pemberton Wine Region. The area's vineyards produce highly regarded Pinot Noir and Chardonnay thanks to the cool microclimate created by the surrounding forests. Estates offer tastings and some provide guided tours of their timber cellars.
Evening stroll at Big Brook Dam. The reservoir encircled by karri forest is the locals' favourite swimming spot. The cool water and silent canopy at dusk offer perfect serenity after a day's hiking.

places.name: Perth

visits.text: Kings Park covers 400 hectares atop Mount Eliza, overlooking the Swan River and Perth's skyline. Founded in 1872, it's one of the world's largest urban parks. Its botanic garden shelters over 3,000 species of Western Australian native plants.

Fremantle, 20 km southwest, is Perth's historic port, heritage-listed for its preserved Victorian architecture. Founded in 1829, its honey-coloured limestone streets concentrate the 1855 colonial prison, the 1897 covered market and craft breweries.

The Art Gallery of Western Australia, founded in 1895, holds the state's largest collection of Australian art. Its permanent rooms cover desert Aboriginal art from the west, colonial paintings and international contemporary art.

activities.text: Cruise down the Swan River from central Perth to Fremantle by public ferry (45 min). The view over the Heirisson Island wetlands and riverside parklands is more scenic than driving.

Fremantle Markets (open Fri-Sun) is Perth's liveliest market, in an 1897 covered market building. Fresh produce, Swan Valley wines, local crafts and food stalls jostle beneath the steel-and-glass canopy.

Fremantle Prison (1855) is UNESCO World Heritage-listed since 2010 among the Australian Convict Sites. Guided tours reconstruct 19th-century prison life, including the underground solitary confinement cells.

places.name: Port Campbell

visits.text: The Great Ocean Road, listed as Australian National Heritage, winds 243 km along Victoria's coast between Torquay and Allansford. Built between 1919 and 1932 by First World War veterans as a living memorial, it's dedicated to fallen soldiers—the world's largest war memorial. The road traces the Port Campbell Limestone cliffs, deposited in a subtropical marine environment between 14 and 7 million years ago.

The Twelve Apostles are isolated limestone stacks rising from the Southern Ocean, sculpted by erosion over millions of years: cliffs are pierced to form caves, then arches, then collapse leaving pillars standing. Despite their name, there were never twelve—nine originally, seven remain today after collapses in 2005 and 2009. The tallest reach 45 meters. The site receives over 2 million visitors annually.

Loch Ard Gorge, three minutes from the Apostles, is a steep-sided gorge named after the clipper ship Loch Ard that wrecked here in 1878: 54 people perished, only two young 18-year-old survivors—Tom Pearce and Eva Carmichael—managed to swim to the gorge. Interpretive panels recount the shipwreck story along the marked trails hugging the walls. Gibson Steps, two minutes by car, descends 86 steps to a beach framed by 70-meter cliffs.

activities.text: Stop at Bells Beach in Torquay, marking the start of the Great Ocean Road. It's one of Australia's most famous surf breaks, site of the Rip Curl Pro since 1973—the World Surf League's longest-running competition. Plan for coffee in Lorne (halfway) at one of the many beachfront cafés.

Optional detour into Great Otway National Park to observe wild koalas in their natural eucalyptus habitat, particularly along Cape Otway Road (heavy traffic in late afternoon). Cape Otway Lighthouse (1848, mainland Australia's oldest operating lighthouse) offers panoramic views over Bass Strait.

visits.text: Wadjemup (Rottnest Island) - 'place where spirits cross the water' in Noongar language - was an island connected to the mainland until 7,000 years ago. Dutch explorer Willem de Vlamingh named it Rottenest ('rats' nest') in 1696, having mistaken the quokkas for giant rodents.

The island counts 63 beaches, 20 bays and 12 inland salt lakes. Its turquoise waters shelter coral reefs and marked underwater trails (snorkeling trail) accessible directly from shore without needing a boat.

Over 10,000 quokkas live on Rottnest - the world's largest wild population of this vulnerable marsupial. Free from natural predators on the island, they're active during the day, often approaching humans, but it's illegal to feed or touch them under penalty of fines.

activities.text: Hire a bike as soon as you arrive on the ferry: it's the only personal transport on the island (private cars are banned). A 22 km circuit loops the island, with stops at bays, Cathedral Rocks and the Cape Vlamingh lighthouse.

Snorkel the marked underwater trails at The Basin or Little Armstrong Bay: corals, tropical fish and dolphins are regularly present. In season (September-December), humpback whales cruise past the island.
A Wadjuk Noongar guide from the Rottnest Island Authority offers cultural walks tracing 30,000 years of Aboriginal occupation of Wadjemup and the painful history of the Aboriginal prison (1839-1903).

days_plan.itin_title: Australia Grand Tour: Natural Wonders and Aboriginal Heritage

places.name: Sydney

visits.text: Sydney Opera House (1973, UNESCO 2007) is the work of Danish architect Jørn Utzon, winner of an international competition in 1957—he received the Pritzker Prize in 2003 for this masterpiece. Its white shells, inspired according to Utzon by segments of a perfect sphere, are covered with over one million self-cleaning Swedish tiles. The building hosts more than 1,500 performances and 1.2 million spectators annually.

Sydney Harbour Bridge (1932) is the world's widest steel arch bridge (49 m) and the fifth longest—its construction employed 1,400 workers over eight years. BridgeClimb offers guided ascent to the arch summit at 134 m above the harbor in 3.5 hours. From Pyrmont Bridge or Circular Quay ferries, the simultaneous view of the bridge and Opera House is Australia's iconic image.

The Rocks, Sydney's first European quarter founded in 1788, brings together the city's oldest stone constructions. The cobbled laneways, Victorian pubs like the Hero of Waterloo (1843) and the Mercantile Hotel, and converted warehouses trace the history of the British penal colony. The weekend market beneath white tents is one of Sydney's busiest.

Bondi Beach, 8 km from the city center, is Australia's most famous beach: 1 km of golden sand open to the Pacific Ocean, with consistent swells monitored by the legendary lifeguards in yellow shorts since 1907. The Bondi to Coogee Coastal Walk (6 km, 2h) follows the cliffs past several wild beaches and the famous Bondi Icebergs rock pools.

activities.text: Ferry from Circular Quay to Manly (30 min harbor crossing)—one of the world's most beautiful ferry journeys with views of the Opera House and bridge. Manly Beach is a surf beach facing the Pacific Ocean, separated from the calm harbor waters by only a few hundred meters. The Manly Scenic Walkway follows the harbor shoreline for 10 km to Spit Bridge.

Excursion to the Blue Mountains (100 km west of Sydney, 2h each way), a UNESCO World Heritage National Park. Echo Point at Katoomba offers the most famous view of the Three Sisters—three sandstone pillars at 906, 918 and 922 meters formed 200 million years ago during the Triassic. Gundungurra Dreamtime legend tells of three sisters transformed to stone for protection during a tribal battle. Scenic World offers the world's steepest railway (52°) and the panoramic Skyway cableway.

places.name: Townsville

visits.text: Townsville is Australia's largest tropical city outside a capital with 200,000 residents, and the main gateway to the central Great Barrier Reef sector. Castle Hill, a 286-meter granite monolith dominating the city, is accessible on foot (2 km, 45 min) or by car via a winding road. From the summit: 360° panorama over Townsville, the port, Magnetic Island and the Great Barrier Reef.

Reef HQ Aquarium is the world's largest living coral reef aquarium, with 2.5 million liters of seawater and over 130 coral species and 120 fish species in their reconstructed natural environment. An underwater walkway allows observation of zebra sharks, sea turtles and humphead wrasse from inside the reef. Active scientific research center on coral rehabilitation.

activities.text: Excursion to Magnetic Island (20 min ferry from Townsville city center). This 50% national park island harbors wild koalas in the eucalyptus along Forts Walk (3 km, 1.5h, easy)—one of Queensland's best wild koala observations without enclosures. Horseshoe Bay and Alma Bay beaches offer quality snorkeling.

places.name: Walpole

visits.text: The Valley of the Giants Tree Top Walk is a 600-metre steel walkway suspended up to 40 metres above the ground in the canopy of tingle trees—giant eucalypts endemic to the Walpole area. These trees can reach 75 metres in height, with some base circumferences of 15 metres. The light walkway, built on steel pylons with no ground foundations, sways gently in the wind just like the trees themselves. The botanical origins of this forest stretch back 65 million years to the supercontinent Gondwana.

The Ancient Empire Walk (650 metres, free entry) plunges to ground level among the monumental trunks of red tingle trees, some 400 years old. The wooden boardwalk passes between—and sometimes through—trunks whose time-hollowed bases form natural doorways. The star of this trail is 'Grandma Tingle', whose hollow trunk is one of the forest's most impressive.

Nornalup Inlet is a dark, calm-water estuary encircled by tingle and karri forests—a striking contrast to the roar of the nearby Southern Ocean. Accessible from Nornalup village, the inlet attracts black swans, wading birds, and in winter, dolphins that venture upstream from the coast.

Conspicuous Cliff is a sheer granite promontory above the Southern Ocean, a few kilometres from Walpole. From the lookout, the panorama sweeps across kilometres of wild coastline without a single house or boat in sight—one of the most solitary views on the Australian coast. The cliff is reached by a short signposted walk from the car park.

activities.text: Combine the Tree Top Walk and Ancient Empire Walk in a single visit. Perfect timing: ascend into the canopy at opening (9 am) before the crowds arrive, then descend for the ground-level trail. Allow about 1.5 hours for both circuits.

Kayak or canoe on Nornalup Inlet at sunrise. The tranquil, tannin-stained waters bordered by dense forest and granite cliffs offer an extraordinary setting for silent paddling among the wildlife. Rentals available in Walpole.

visits.text: Bandilngan (Windjana Gorge): the Lennard River has carved 3.5 km through the Napier Range, a 375-million-year-old fossilized coral reef. Limestone walls rise 100 meters high. Marine fossils are visible embedded in the rock face.

Dimalurru (Tunnel Creek) is Western Australia's oldest cave system. Water has carved a 750-meter tunnel through the Napier Range — reaching 20 meters high and 15 meters wide in places. This is where Jandamarra, Bunuba resistance leader, took refuge until his death in 1897.

The ruins of Lillimilura Police Station, built from local limestone in 1884 for the King Sound Pastoral Company, can be visited at the park edge. The site is intimately connected to Jandamarra's story.

activities.text: Walk the Gorge Trail (1.9 km return) deep into the gorge along the freshwater pools. Allow an hour: around each bend, freshwater crocodiles bask on the banks, utterly indifferent to visitors.

Enter Tunnel Creek with a powerful torch. You'll wade through calf-deep pools, brush past stalactites, and encounter several bat species including the rare Ghost Bat. The exit at the far end opens onto a sunlit clearing.
At evening in Windjana Gorge campground, watch flying foxes and corellas settle in massive numbers in the trees along the water — a unique audio-visual spectacle.
